Details: Both outputs are well-structured PySpark pytest scripts that follow testing best practices. Agent 1 focuses on data transformation and aggregation testing with 5 comprehensive test cases, while Agent 2 addresses CSV data loading and SAP BW integration with 4 test cases. Both demonstrate proper fixture usage, error handling, and assertion patterns. The main difference lies in business context rather than technical implementation quality.

Both scripts serve the same high-level purpose of PySpark unit testing but address completely different business scenarios. Agent 1 tests data transformation logic with status assignment based on value thresholds, while Agent 2 tests CSV data loading into SAP BW tables. The testing methodologies and pytest patterns are semantically aligned, but the core business logic being tested is fundamentally different.

Both scripts follow nearly identical structural patterns: SparkSession fixture setup, helper functions, and multiple test functions with clear naming conventions. Both use proper pytest decorators, similar data setup approaches, and consistent assertion patterns. The main structural difference is in the number of test cases (5 vs 4) and specific helper function implementations.

Agent 1 script has proper imports, valid syntax, and consistent variable usage. Minor issue: the transform_gl_data function could benefit from explicit return type annotation. All test functions are properly structured with valid assertions.

Agent 2 script has more complex import handling with dynamic module loading which could be fragile. The monkeypatching approach is sophisticated but introduces complexity. Some hardcoded paths and assumptions about module structure could cause runtime issues.
Both scripts demonstrate high syntactic correctness with proper pytest structure, valid PySpark operations, and appropriate error handling patterns.
Consider adding type annotations to helper functions and implementing more comprehensive boundary testing for edge cases in data transformation logic.

Simplify the dynamic module import approach and consider using more robust path handling. The monkeypatching strategy is sophisticated but could be made more maintainable with clearer separation of concerns.

Both scripts would benefit from additional documentation comments explaining the business context and test scenarios. Consider standardizing the test data setup patterns and adding performance benchmarking for larger datasets.
